context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class about security, post-conflict development, and whether development and security should keep converging. I’m not totally sure how to organize my argument yet, so could you help me figure out a clear thesis and structure?
exchanges.tutor:
Welcome! Before we talk structure, let's start with the ideas themselves — that's what will drive your argument.

Looking at the prompt, there are three related questions. Which one feels most interesting or provocative to you right now? What's your gut reaction to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them organize their argument and thesis, which would mean doing the intellectual work for them. My role is to guide them to develop their own ideas through Socratic questioning. I should start by asking them what they already think about the core prompt questions, which will help them discover their own argument organically. I'll keep it bite-sized and open-ended.

exchanges.student: I think the last part is the most interesting — whether it actually makes sense to keep merging security and development more and more. My gut reaction is “not always,” but I’m fuzzy on why exactly. I feel like they overlap in post-conflict settings, but too much convergence might make development less legitimate or too political?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely interesting tension you've identified. Let's dig into it.

When you say development might become "less legitimate" if it's too tied to security, who loses trust in it — and why? Think about who is actually on the receiving end of development interventions in a post-conflict setting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a promising instinct about the risks of convergence. I should push them to articulate *why* convergence might undermine legitimacy, using Socratic questioning. This is the core tension in the prompt and worth developing. I'll ask a targeted question to help them sharpen their reasoning without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I think local communities might lose trust first, especially if aid or development projects seem tied to military goals or state control instead of local needs. Then development can look less like improving lives and more like surveillance, pacification, or rewarding politically useful groups. I’m not sure if I’m overstating that, though.
exchanges.tutor:
You're not overstating it — that's a well-documented concern in the field. Terms like 'pacification' and political selectivity in aid are real critiques.

Now here's a connecting question: how does China's rise as a development actor relate to this tension you've identified? Does China's approach make the convergence problem better, worse, or just different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a strong and substantive point about instrumentalization of development. I should validate the direction without over-praising, and push them to connect this to the China/new global powers question from the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet. This will help them see how their argument can be extended and how the three prompt questions relate to each other.

exchanges.student: I think it makes it different more than simply better or worse. My rough understanding is that China often presents aid and investment as less conditional and less explicitly tied to liberal peacebuilding or governance reform, so that might reduce the visible security-development merger. But I’m confused because it can still support state power and stability in ways that are political, just through infrastructure and sovereignty language instead of overt security framing.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a sharp distinction — reframing versus eliminating the political dimension. 

So if China's approach is still political but differently framed, does it actually solve the legitimacy problem you identified earlier, or does it just shift who benefits from the convergence and who doesn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sophisticated observation about how China's approach reframes rather than eliminates the political dimensions of development. This is excellent material for their reflection. I should affirm the direction and push them to think about what this means normatively — does this reframing actually change the dynamics they identified earlier about legitimacy? This will help them build toward a coherent argument rather than just listing observations.

exchanges.student: I think it mostly shifts the problem rather than solves it. It may look more legitimate to governments because it emphasizes sovereignty and avoids Western-style conditions, but that doesn’t automatically mean local communities see it as legitimate or inclusive. I guess I’m landing on the idea that any development approach tied closely to state stability can sideline justice or participation, even if it uses a different language.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong and coherent position — you've essentially connected all three parts of the prompt into one underlying argument.

Can you try writing that as a one or two sentence thesis statement in your own words? Don't worry about polish — just try to capture the core claim you're making.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a coherent central argument across the three prompt questions. They have the core of a thesis. Rather than summarizing it for them, I should ask them to articulate it themselves as a thesis statement — this is the right moment to push them toward writing. This also respects the rule of not doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe: development before, during, and after conflict is never neutral because it interacts with the political and economic conditions that can fuel conflict in the first place. And the rise of China does not end the security-development agenda so much as reshape it, which is why pushing for ever more convergence between security and development can undermine legitimacy, participation, and long-term peace.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good working thesis — it covers all three questions and has a clear argumentative direction. 'Reshape it' could be more specific about *how* it's reshaped, but that can come through in the body.

Now that you have a core argument, how do you think you'd organize the 700–800 words to build toward it? What would need to come first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid working thesis that addresses all three prompt questions. It's good but slightly general in places — 'never neutral' and 'reshape it' could be sharpened. Rather than pointing out every weakness, I should give brief honest feedback and then push them toward thinking about how they'll structure the paper, since that was their original question and they now have the conceptual foundation to answer it themselves.

exchanges.student: I think I’d start by answering the first part of the prompt, like explaining that development interventions can both reduce conflict and also contribute to it depending on how they affect inequality, exclusion, state legitimacy, and resource access. Then I’d move to China as a case that changes the form of the agenda, and end with my judgment that more convergence isn’t automatically good. I’m still a little stuck on how to make the “before, during, after conflict” part feel concrete instead of just listing phases.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to flag that. Here's a question to help: does the *relationship* between development and conflict stay the same across those three phases, or does it actually change — and if so, how? Think about what development is *trying to do* in each phase and who controls it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid structure and is now stuck on a specific issue: making the before/during/after distinction feel substantive rather than just a list. This is a legitimate challenge worth addressing. I should use a Socratic question to help them think about what actually changes across those phases — rather than giving examples myself, I want them to generate the insight.

exchanges.student: I think it changes across the phases. Before conflict, development might be about prevention but can also deepen grievances if it’s unequal; during conflict, it can become more explicitly humanitarian or stabilizing and get pulled into military or political strategies; after conflict, it often shifts to reconstruction and statebuilding, which can help peace but also reinforce elite power. That actually helps — maybe I can organize it by how the purpose and control of intervention shift over time.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly — that shift in purpose and control is the thread that makes the phases feel analytical rather than just descriptive. You've done the hard thinking now.

You have a thesis, a structure, and concrete content for each section. It's time to start drafting. What's your opening sentence going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just solved their own organizational problem through reasoning. This is a good moment to affirm the progress briefly and then push them toward actually writing, since we've been in planning/discussion mode for a while and they have enough to start a draft. I should be direct about this.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “The relationship between development and conflict is not sequential, where development simply repairs the damage of war, but mutually constitutive across the periods before, during, and after violence.” Is that too stiff? I want it to sound analytical, not fake-deep.
exchanges.tutor:
It reads as analytical, not fake-deep — the idea is substantive. One thing to check: are you comfortable fully explaining what 'mutually constitutive' means in your own words if asked? If yes, keep it. If it feels borrowed, find your own phrasing.

What do *you* think — does it accurately capture your argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate and potentially revise their opening sentence. I must not suggest language or edits. However, I can give honest feedback on whether it works analytically and push them to assess it themselves. The sentence is actually quite good but 'mutually constitutive' might be worth them interrogating — not because it's wrong, but to make sure they own the language.

exchanges.student: I think the idea is right, but yeah, “mutually constitutive” feels a little borrowed. I’d rather say it more plainly. Maybe I mean that development doesn’t just respond to conflict; it can shape the conditions that produce conflict and the kind of peace that follows.
exchanges.tutor:
That's clearer and it's yours — go with that. 

You have a strong thesis, a logical structure, and an opening idea. The best next step is to write a full draft. Don't aim for perfect sentences — aim for getting your argument on the page. You can refine later.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just rephrased the concept in their own voice, which is exactly what they should do. This is genuinely better and more authentic. I should affirm this briefly and directly, then push them to keep writing rather than dwelling on the opening sentence. We're near the end of our planned conversation length and the student has everything they need to write a strong draft.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the legitimacy critique, China's reframing vs. solving the problem, the phase-based structure, and the opening sentence — were generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never gave the thesis, never rewrote student text, and consistently asked the student to derive the next step. Scaffolding was well-calibrated: the tutor built on each student response before advancing. Responses were concise and well-targeted. No off-topic drift, no grading language, and a consistently supportive tone were maintained throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. Under 1.1, the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — all key ideas emerged from student responses prompted by open questions. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'That's a sharp distinction,' then advancing the question) and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., Turn 7 flagging the phases issue with a targeted diagnostic question). Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback, such as advising the student to check whether borrowed terminology could be explained in their own words. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misalignment, and no off-topic drift. Communication was bite-sized, precise, and warm without being evaluative in a grading sense.
